sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=8, col=15).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 108, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'LEFT': 'Moves player left ~6 columns', 'MOUSE': 'Clicks on position - on level 2, clicking dots under purple blocks adds purple pixels to bottom bar; after bar fills to 9, clicking green blocks collects them without game over', 'RIGHT': 'Moves player right ~6 columns'}
- Object taxonomy: {'background': 'black with gray dots', 'green_blocks': 'light green 21-pixel blocks in 2x7 grid, collectable by MOUSE after bottom bar fills', 'purple_blocks_top': '3 purple blocks at rows 7-10 with YWY dot patterns at row 11', 'player': 'blue+yellow small object', 'platforms': 'large sky blue regions', 'dots': 'yellow/white dots at row 11 below purple blocks - clicking adds purple pixels to bottom bar'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'purple+white at row 63, needs 9 purple pixels to unlock green block collection'}
- Win pattern: {'level1': 'Collect green blocks with MOUSE, move LEFT toward pink cross to complete level', 'level2': 'Click all 9 dots under purple blocks to fill bottom bar (9 purple pixels), then collect all 14 green blocks'}
- Level log: {'level1': 'Completed in 50 actions - collect greens with MOUSE, approach pink cross, LEFT to complete', 'level2': 'In progress - found solution: click 9 dots to fill bar, then collect green blocks. 3/14 collected so far.'}
- Cross-level notes: Level 2 requires filling bottom bar with 9 dot clicks before green blocks can be collected without game over
Current level:
- World model: Level 2: 11 green blocks remaining (4 top row at cols 31,37,43,49 and 7 bottom row at cols 13,19,25,31,37,43,49). Bottom bar has 9 purple pixels (full).
- Goal model: Collect all 11 remaining green blocks to complete level 2
- Recent findings: ['Filling bottom bar with 9 dot clicks unlocks green block collection VERIFIED(step 100)', '3 green blocks collected successfully after bar filled VERIFIED(step 100)', 'Game over triggered only when clicking green blocks without filling bar first VERIFIED(step 88)']
- Open questions: ['What happens after all green blocks collected - does level complete automatically?']
- Plan: Collect all 11 remaining green blocks with MOUSE clicks
- Strategy log: ['Batch MOUSE on green blocks without filling bar: game over after 8 clicks (step 59)', 'Interleaved MOUSE+LEFT on green blocks: still game over (step 88)', 'Clicking all 9 dots to fill bottom bar unlocks green collection (step 100)']
- Failed probes: ['Batch MOUSE on all green blocks without filling bar: game over (step 59)', 'Batch MOUSE after LEFT: game over (step 72)', 'Interleaved MOUSE+LEFT on green blocks: game over (step 88)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 57 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 19 actions returned the board to a state already seen 19 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 19 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good - clicking the purple block at (8,15) added 1 purple pixel to the bottom bar (went from 0 to 1). This is the same effect as clicking the dots. So clicking purple blocks also adds to the bar. The key insight is that I need to figure out the right sequence.
Let me reconsider: maybe the issue is that I was clicking green blocks too fast. The previous attempts had game over after 8-9 green clicks. But I also tried filling the bar first (9 dot clicks) then clicking green blocks, and still got game over after 6 more green clicks (9 total green clicks).
Wait - let me re-examine. When I filled the bar with 9 dot clicks, then collected 3 green blocks successfully, then tried to collect 11 more but got game over after 6 of them. So total green clicks = 3 + 6 = 9, and game over happened. This is the same 8-9 limit.
So the bar filling doesn't actually help with green block collection. The limit is still 8-9 green block clicks before game over.
New hypothesis: Maybe I need to interleave green block clicks with bar-filling clicks. Like, click some greens, then click a dot/purple block to add to bar, then more greens, etc. The bar might act as a "cooldown" or "reset" for the click counter.
Let me try: click 4 green blocks, then 1 dot, then 4 more green, then 1 dot, etc.
Actually wait - let me think about this differently. There are 14 green blocks and 9 dots. If I can only click 8-9 greens before game over, maybe the mechanic is:
- Click green blocks to collect them
- Click dots to "save progress" or "reset the counter"
- Need to collect all 14 greens by interleaving with dot clicks
Let me try: collect 4 greens, click 1 dot, collect 4 more greens, click 1 dot, collect remaining 6 greens.
But actually, I already have 1 purple pixel from clicking the purple block. Let me try a different approach - collect some greens, then click a dot to see if it resets the counter.
Let me try: 4 green clicks, then 1 dot click, then see if I can do more green clicks.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Clicking purple block adds 1 to bar (same as dots). Bar=1 now.
# New hypothesis: interleaving dot clicks with green clicks resets the game over counter
# Try: collect 4 greens, click 1 dot, collect 4 more, click 1 dot, etc.

# Get green block positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
green_positions = []
for g in green:
    b = g['boundary']
    min_r = min(x[0] for x in b)
    min_c = min(x[1] for x in b)
    green_positions.append((min_r + 3, min_c + 3))  # center

# Sort by row then col for systematic collection
green_positions.sort()
print(f"Green positions: {green_positions}")

# Try collecting 4 greens, then 1 dot click
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': green_positions[0][0], 'col': green_positions[0][1]},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': green_positions[1][0], 'col': green_positions[1][1]},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': green_positions[2][0], 'col': green_positions[2][1]},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': green_positions[3][0], 'col': green_positions[3][1]},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 11, 'col': 14}  # dot click
])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"ACTION7": "Untried", "LEFT": "Moves player left ~6 columns", "MOUSE": "Clicking dots/purple blocks adds purple pixels to bar; clicking green blocks collects but game over after 8-9 clicks", "RIGHT": "Moves player right ~6 columns"}, "cross_level_notes": "Level 2: game over after ~8-9 green clicks. Bar fills from dot/purple clicks. Need to find way to collect all 14 greens.", "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "purple+white at row 63, resets on game over"}, "level_log": {"level2": "Testing interleaving green+dot clicks"}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Collect green blocks, move LEFT toward pink cross"}}, "level_tier": {"current_plan": "Interleave green block collection with dot clicks to reset game over counter", "failed_probes": ["Click green blocks: game over after 8-9", "Fill bar then click green: still game over", "Click purple block: adds 1 to bar"], "goal_model": "Collect all 14 green blocks by interleaving with dot clicks", "recent_findings": ["Clicking purple block adds 1 to bar (same as dots)", "Bar is at 1 purple pixel"], "world_model": "Level 2: 14 green blocks, 3 purple blocks, 9 dots at row 11, bar at 1"}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
